IV. Requirements for Plagiarism Detection

(1) A similarity rate below 10% is considered passing; appropriate revisions should be made based on the detection report.
(2) A similarity rate between 10% and 20% is considered passing after revision; the supervising instructor must oversee and confirm the revisions.
(3) A similarity rate above 20% is considered failing; substantial revisions are required, followed by another round of plagiarism detection.
(4) If the thesis similarity rate does not meet the standard and requires revision and re‑detection, please contact the Undergraduate Academic Office before the defense. A similarity rate below 10% is required to proceed to the defense. If the defense committee determines that the thesis may be defended, it shall be regarded as passing, but the student or the supervising instructor must submit a written explanation. The explanation will be confirmed by the defense committee and filed with the Undergraduate Academic Office. Students must attach importance to revising their theses after plagiarism detection and promptly send the plagiarism report and the full‑text report to their supervising instructor. The AIGC detection results are for reference only, serving as a basis for instructors and defense committee members to assess the student’s mastery of relevant professional knowledge and skills. They are not mandatory evaluation criteria and are not directly linked to whether the thesis passes.
policy_summary:
To strengthen academic integrity education, regulate the scientific and reasonable use of generative artificial intelligence technology by undergraduate students in their graduation theses (designs), and prevent academic misconduct, the university has decided to provide AIGC detection alongside plagiarism detection for the 2025 undergraduate graduation theses (designs). Specific arrangements are as follows.

I. Detection Platform

The CNKI plagiarism detection and AIGC detection functions have been integrated into the Undergraduate Academic Management Information Service Platform (website: zdbk.zju.edu.cn, hereinafter referred to as the Academic System). Students can directly conduct graduation thesis plagiarism detection and AIGC detection through the Academic System.

policy_summary:
Article 1 The guidance aim to integrate the standard requirements of higher education into the whole life cycle of "AI+ education and teaching" reform, covering various processes such as scenario analysis, design and development, application deployment, evaluation and feedback, adjustment and maintenance, management optimization, etc. By innovating evaluation systems and standards, these guidances promote efficiency, credibility, fairness, and inclusiveness in all processes, effectively preventing risks such as technical incompetence, educational differentiation, algorithmic discrimination, privacy breaches, data insecurity, and cyber threats.

Article 2 These guidances apply to all stakeholders in the "AI + Education and Teaching" reform at SJTU, namely "teachers," "students," "machines" (AI technology systems), and the "environment" (educational and teaching ecological environment), including teaching management and assistant staff, inter-university cooperators, and social resource providers.

Article 3 While complying with domestic and international AI laws and guidances, Shanghai Jiao Tong University encourages active exploration of the use of AI in the field of higher education and teaching, promotes technology empowerment in various scenarios, promotes technology empowerment and risk prevention in various scenarios, constructs knowledge production models and new educational systems that meet the needs of the new era, and empowers students to enhance their comprehensive literacy.

Article 4 The term "Artificial Intelligence (AI)" in the guidances refers to the simulation, extension, or expansion of human intelligence through methods such as environmental perception, knowledge acquisition, deduction, and reasoning, including but not limited to various technical routes such as discriminative, generative, data-driven, and knowledge graph-driven AI. The term "Human Intelligence (HI)" refers to the unique ability to understand, apply, and create knowledge. These guidances strive to promote the combination of "AI + HI" to achieve the goal of benign human-machine collaboration.
Article 5 All parties involved in "AI+ Education and teaching" at Shanghai Jiao Tong University shall follow the following basic principles:
(1) Human-Centered and Learning-Focused Technology: Centering on the educational philosophy of "making every student better," develop and apply AI technologies that benefit students' intellectual growth and teachers' instructional and educational efforts. Regularly assess the use situations of tools by teachers' and students', to ensure that technology application assists teaching goals and avoids complete substitution or singlefaceted reliance on teaching tasks.
(2) Legal, Compliant, Reliable, and Trustworthy Technology: AI services or products developed and applied in educational and teaching reforms should comply with the legal and regulatory requirements, ensuring fairness, clear responsibility, transparency, and traceability. AI applications should establish responsible entities and accountability processes.
(3) Respect for Diversity and Promotion of Open Collaboration: Respect the diversity of disciplines and knowledge in higher education, promote knowledge integration and innovation, explore autonomous and spontaneous personalized learning modes based on disciplinary characteristics, and pay attention to minority groups or interdisciplinary students to ensure widespread accessibility to shared educational resources.
(4) Encourage Experimental Reform and Tolerate Exploratory Innovation: Fully leverage the initiative and enthusiasm of teachers and students, encourage extensive AI experiments in all aspects and fields of education and teaching, explore optimization schemes for "AI + HI" in different scenarios, iteratively improve with tolerance for errors, and do not disrupt the existing teaching order while adhering to academic norms, teaching bottom lines, and ethical boundaries.
(5) Enhance Ethical Literacy and Encourage Agile Governance: Popularize knowledge on AI ethical governance through educational and teaching activities, agilely respond to potential risks, gradually improve governance norms, establish emergency response plans for different application scenarios, and construct a rapid response mechanism for teachers and students.
Article 6 According to the differences in application risks, these guidances classify AI applications in the field of higher education and teaching into four types: Prohibited Use, Limited Use, Encouraged Use, and Open Use, thereby promoting a multi-level reform of "AI + Education and Teaching."
1. Prohibited Use: Refers to scenarios or links with high risks or that teachers and students have unanimously opposed to upon thorough discussion. Scenarios prohibited from use should be clearly listed in the "AI Application Prohibition List" of SJTU, updated timely through the internal platform, and publicly disclosed to ensure that teachers and students are informed and comply accordingly.
2. Limited Use: Refers to scenarios or links with certain risks that require restricted use under specific conditions based on risk assessments as well as teacher-student discussions. Limited use should be dynamically assessed based on the following reference indicators:
 Privacy impact: Potential threat to the protection of personal information of teachers and students;
 Impact on learning effectiveness: Possible negative impact on teaching objectives and learning outcomes;
 Safety compliance: Whether it complies with relevant laws, guidances, and norms of the university.
Specific constraints should be set for limited-use scenarios, and reassessments should be conducted regularly.
3. Encouraged Use: Refers to scenarios or links with low risks or that teachers and students have reached a high consensus through discussion to promote application. AI tools or technologies encouraged for use need to pass the evaluation process of SJTU to ensure they meet educational and teaching goals, ethical norms, and technical reliability requirements.
4. Open Use: Refers to scenarios or cases with near-zero risks or that teachers and students have unanimously agreed on through discussion for wide use. AI technologies for open use should guarantee fairness, widely benefit teachers and students, and be promoted through the platforms of SJTU.
Furthermore, for scenarios or processes where disagreements persist between teachers and students after thorough discussion, an arbitration or review process can be introduced. The arbitration will be made based on technical evaluations, risk analyses, and educational objectives by the AI Education and Teaching Reform Governance Committee. Then, the committee will finalize the use list.
Article 7 The requirements for the standardized use of AI applications in the field of higher education and teaching cover different processes of before, during and after, including commitment, filing, impact assessment, risk recording and management, transparency and explainability, stakeholder consultation and other mechanisms and measures. For different types of AI applications, each scenario in the field of higher education and teaching should develop and improve the corresponding standard use requirements.

The original text discusses principles, risk categories, and governance processes but does not mention any named commercial or proprietary software (e.g., "CNKI detection system," "Turnitin AI") that must be used to check for AI-generated content in academic work. The focus is on ethical assessment and risk management, not on mandating a specific technical detection instrument.

linked_to_pass = 0: AIGC detection results (or compliance with these guidelines) are not directly linked to thesis approval or degree conferral in the provided text. The original text positions the guidelines as a means to "promote efficiency, credibility, fairness, and inclusiveness" and "construct... new educational systems." While it establishes an arbitration committee, it does not state that violations will result in actions like "disqualification from defense" or "degree revocation." The consequences appear geared towards governance and risk mitigation within the educational process, not as a direct determinant of academic graduation status.

policy_summary:
Fudan University Regulations on the Use of AI Tools in Undergraduate Theses (Designs) (Trial)

Article 1: Purpose and Basis
To standardize the use of AI tools in undergraduate theses (designs), ensure academic integrity, improve teaching quality, and prevent academic misconduct, these regulations are formulated in accordance with Article 6 of the Fudan University Regulations on Undergraduate Theses (Designs).

Article 2: Definitions
AI tools referred to in these regulations include Generative Artificial Intelligence (Generative AI, referred to as "GenAI" or "Generative AI") and Artificial Intelligence Auxiliary Tools (referred to as "AI Auxiliary Tools").

Generative AI: Refers to tools that use artificial intelligence technology to generate content such as text, images, and audio, e.g., ChatGPT, GPTs, ERNIE Bot, etc.

AI Auxiliary Tools: Refers to tools that use artificial intelligence technology to assist in tasks such as language polishing, data analysis, and chart creation.

Article 3: Permitted Scope of Use
Principle: With the consent of the supervising instructor and provided that the content generated by AI tools does not affect the assessment of the student's innovative abilities in the thesis (design), the author may use AI tools within the following scope:

Literature Search and Organization: AI tools may be used for literature retrieval, keyword recommendation, and literature management, but the authenticity and reliability of cited references must be ensured.

Chart Type Recommendation and Auxiliary Chart Creation: AI tools may be used to recommend statistical chart types and assist in chart creation. However, they must not be used to generate or modify original research data or key research charts, nor for theses (designs) where the chart itself is part of the assessment (e.g., artistic design). The originality of the final chart must be ensured.

Non-Innovative Method Assistance with AI Tools: When research methods are not part of the innovative content of the thesis (design), AI tools may be used to assist in programming code writing, debugging, and error detection, as well as in screening and recommending research methods such as statistical methods, experimental methods, and survey methods. The logic, accuracy, scientific validity, and maintainability of the final code or research methods must be ensured, and all code or research methods must be reviewed and tested by the author.

Reference Formatting: AI tools may be used to standardize and automatically sort reference formats, but the generated content must be verified.

Article 4: Prohibited Scope of Use

Research Design and Data Analysis: The use of AI tools is prohibited for research proposal design, innovative method design, algorithm (model) framework construction, thesis (design) structure design, research (design) topic selection, summary of research (design) significance and innovation, research hypothesis formulation, data analysis, result analysis and discussion, and conclusion summarization.

Original Data Collection: The use of AI tools to generate or modify original data in undergraduate theses (designs), such as experimental data, statistical data, or field survey data, is prohibited. Unless AI technology itself is the subject of the research (design), its original data must be generated by AI algorithms.

Result Images and Key Illustration Creation: The use of AI tools to generate or modify original or experimental result images, graphics, and illustrations in undergraduate theses (designs) is prohibited. Unless AI technology itself is part of the research design with reproducible methods, and this is explicitly stated in the methodology section of the thesis.

Thesis Writing: Direct use of AI tools to generate the main text, acknowledgments, or other components of undergraduate theses (designs) is prohibited. AI tools may assist students in learning how to write a thesis (design) but must not replace the author's final independent creation. Language expression ability remains a key focus of thesis (design) assessment. The use of AI tools for language polishing and translation is prohibited.

Defense and Review: During defense or internal thesis review, defense committee members and reviewers are prohibited from using any AI tools to evaluate students' undergraduate theses (designs), including but not limited to using AI tools to summarize the core content of the thesis or generate review comments.

Confidential Content: For undergraduate theses (designs) involving confidential content, the use of any AI tools and the uploading of any data or images to AI platforms are prohibited.

Article 5: Disclosure and Declaration
When using AI tools, the following information must be clearly disclosed in the undergraduate thesis (design) commitment statement:

The name and version of the AI tool used, as well as the time and specific purpose of use.

The content generated or suggestions provided by the AI tool.

The specific sections of the thesis where the AI tool was used, such as the methodology or literature review sections.

Relevant important materials before AI tool processing must be retained for review and inquiry by the supervising instructor or reviewers regarding the use of AI tools.

Article 6: Responsibility and Authorship

The author bears ultimate responsibility for the content generated using AI tools. AI tools may not be listed as authors or co-authors.

When using AI tools, the originality and academic integrity of the final work must be ensured to avoid academic misconduct.

Article 7: Compliance Process and Responsibility

Students must obtain prior consent from their supervising instructor to use AI tools in their undergraduate theses (designs) and comply with relevant university and departmental regulations during use.

Departments should formulate specific regulations for the use of AI tools in theses (designs) within their disciplines under the framework of these regulations. Guidance on the use of AI tools should be provided when necessary to help instructors and students use these technologies correctly.

For students who use AI tools reasonably and compliantly in their undergraduate theses (designs), supervising instructors, defense committee members, and internal reviewers may implement appropriate supplementary assessment measures during the guidance, defense, and internal review stages to verify whether the student has mastered the relevant professional knowledge and abilities.

Students who violate these regulations in using AI tools may face consequences such as inclusion in the thesis (design) assessment grade, disqualification from defense, or cancellation of passing grades, depending on the severity. Cases constituting academic misconduct (impropriety) will be handled according to relevant university regulations, including disciplinary actions, revocation of degree eligibility, or degree revocation.

Article 8: Supplementary Provisions

These regulations are formulated based on the current stage of AI technology development and will be revised periodically as AI technology evolves.

For assessment papers such as exam papers, term papers, and course papers (designs), these regulations may be referenced in the absence of specific targeted regulations.

The use of AI tools in published academic papers (exchange papers) or academic research activities is governed by separate regulations and does not fall under these provisions.

These regulations take effect from the date of issuance and are interpreted by the Academic Affairs Office.
Rationale:

policy_summary:
III. AIGC Detection

In order to guide students in the proper and standardized use of generative artificial intelligence tools and to ensure the originality and academic integrity of their final academic work, "Weipu AIGC Detection" is made available to schools/departments for this year's graduation theses. The decision to use this detection function is at the discretion of each school/department (please follow the notification from your respective school/department regarding whether detection is required). This service provides students with a single free detection opportunity. The detection results are based on probabilistic analysis of algorithmic models, have technical limitations, and are intended **only as an auxiliary reference for academic norm compliance, not as a definitive basis for judging the originality of a thesis**. AIGC detection can be conducted in two modes: student-initiated detection or centralized upload by the school/department. Access is required through the graduation thesis management system at the dedicated university login address for the Weipu detection system: `https://cloud.fanyu.com/organ/lib/nju`.

Student Account: Student ID Number
Initial Password: nju[Student ID Number] (e.g., nju2025123)

After the detection is completed, both the school/department and the student can view the AIGC detection results within the system. Please note, students **must** apply for record-filing, maintain complete process documentation, and undergo supervision during the use of generative AI tools for their graduation thesis. The final thesis version must include the *Nanjing University Undergraduate Graduation Thesis (Design) Artificial Intelligence Use Record Form*. All documents and operating instructions related to AIGC detection can be found in the attached AIGC detection files.

policy_summary:
V. AIGC (Generative Artificial Intelligence) Detection Requirements

Starting this year, the university will conduct AIGC detection on graduation projects (theses). After uploading the graduation project (thesis) to the "China National Knowledge Infrastructure" (CNKI) detection system, AIGC detection can be performed simultaneously with the conventional plagiarism check. The AIGC detection results, along with the plagiarism check results, will be provided to the respective schools (departments).

Principles for the Use of AIGC in Graduation Projects (Theses):
AIGC may only be used as an auxiliary tool to support students' learning and research processes and must not replace students' independent thinking and original contributions. Students should ensure that content generated by AIGC does not compromise the innovative capabilities, practical skills, or academic value demonstrated in their graduation projects (theses). Students bear ultimate responsibility for content generated by AIGC and must ensure it adheres to academic standards and meets the quality requirements of the graduation project (thesis).

Each school (department) should, based on the above principles and in consideration of their specific disciplinary characteristics, establish individualized AIGC detection thresholds for graduation projects (theses). Results below the threshold may be considered normal technical assistance, while results exceeding the threshold should trigger manual review or an academic ethics investigation.

Schools (departments) and individual disciplines are encouraged to publish specific scenarios categorized as "Prohibited Use," "Limited Use," "Encouraged Use," and "Open Use." It is recommended that the following core chapters involving academic innovation in graduation projects (theses) prohibit the use of AI-generated content: literature review, research design, innovative methodology design, original data collection, theoretical derivation, conclusions, etc. AI assistance is suggested for the following aspects of graduation projects (theses): literature search and organization, recommendation and assistance in creating secondary chart types, AI tool assistance for non-innovative methods, and reference formatting.
Rationale: Check framework is coded as 1: The university explicitly requires AIGC detection for graduation or degree theses, as the original text states that the university will conduct AIGC detection on graduation projects (theses) starting this year, with clear requirements for detection implementation and usage principles, forming a formal mandatory framework; Tool specified is coded as 1: The university explicitly specifies a specific AIGC detection tool (e.g., CNKI AI Check, Turnitin AI detection), with the original text indicating that AIGC detection is performed within the "CNKI (China National Knowledge Infrastructure)" detection system and is synchronized with regular paper duplicate checking; Linked to pass is coded as 1: AIGC detection results directly affect thesis approval, defense eligibility, or degree conferral, as the original text requires colleges (departments) to formulate personalized AIGC detection thresholds, and results exceeding the threshold will trigger manual review or academic ethics investigation, which is directly linked to the compliance and approval of the graduation project (thesis).

Schools may independently select AIGC detection tools and must conduct 100% detection on undergraduate graduation theses (designs), with schools retaining the detection results for future reference. Schools are requested to refer to these ratios and notify the relevant supervisors and students to address graduation theses (designs) with detected AIGC proportions exceeding these thresholds. It is recommended that students within the same school use the same AIGC detection tool to facilitate the collection and comparison of detection results.

Considering the differences across disciplines, the similarity check rate for undergraduate theses at our university is determined independently by each school based on the specific requirements of their respective fields. Students are advised to consult their respective schools for the specific similarity check rate requirements.

. VIPS (Weipu) Paper Detection System

Students are provided with 1 similarity check opportunity + 1 AIGC check opportunity (external to the graduation project system; please refer to Attachment 1 for the external system operation manual). The website is: [https://vpcs.fanyu.com/personal/seu](https://vpcs.fanyu.com/personal/seu), Username: Campus Card Number, Initial Password: a + Student ID (e.g., if the student ID is 01111111, the password is a01111111).

Additionally, upon submitting the final draft in the graduation project system, 2 similarity check opportunities are granted. The system automatically integrates the VIPS paper detection service. After submitting the final thesis, students can click the "Submit for Similarity Check" button, and the system will automatically perform the similarity check and return the detection results and report. Instructors can view the students' paper detection results on the graduation project system interface. For steps and common issues regarding the VIPS similarity check within the system, please refer to Attachment 2: Thesis Submission, Similarity Check Steps, and Common Issues (for Students).

3. Regulating the Use of Artificial Intelligence
   In the process of writing the graduation thesis (design), students should use AI writing tools effectively and reasonably, treating them solely as supplementary aids to ensure the independent creation of the core content of the thesis. 3. Guidelines for the Use of AIGC

In accordance with the requirements outlined in the Notice on Regulating the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence in Graduation Theses (Designs) (Undergraduate Student Notice [2025] No. 33), it is strictly prohibited to directly use generative artificial intelligence (AIGC) tools for uploading confidential theses, writing core content of the thesis, fabricating research data and processes, or ghostwriting entire theses. Any such conduct, once discovered and verified, will be deemed an act of thesis fraud. Pursuant to the Detailed Implementation Rules for Handling Thesis Fraud at China Agricultural University (CAU Graduate Student Notice [2015] No. 1), students who have not yet been awarded a degree will have their degree application eligibility revoked. Those who have already been awarded a degree will have their degree rescinded, and their degree certificate will be annulled.

policy_summary:
To All Faculty and Students,

In response to the advancement of artificial intelligence technology, and in order to regulate the use of AI tools in undergraduate graduation projects (theses) at our university, uphold academic integrity, and enhance the quality of talent cultivation, the following matters are hereby notified in accordance with the Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics Administrative Measures for Undergraduate Graduation Projects (Theses) (Revised) (University Academic Notice [2023] No. 64) and related documents, as well as the university’s practical circumstances:

I. Definition of AI Tools
The AI tools referred to in this notice include generative artificial intelligence (e.g., DeepSeek, ChatGPT, ERNIE Bot, etc.) and auxiliary AI tools (e.g., grammar checkers, data analysis tools, etc.).

II. Fundamental Principles
Legality and Compliance: The use of AI tools in graduation projects (theses) must comply with national laws, regulations, and policies, adhere to academic ethics, and must not infringe upon the intellectual property rights of others.

Auxiliary Use: AI tools may only assist in the research and writing of graduation projects (theses) and must not replace students’ independent thinking. Content generated by AI tools must be clearly distinguished from the student’s own academic contributions and appropriately documented.

Author Responsibility: Students must independently verify content generated or processed by AI tools to ensure the authenticity and reliability of data and information, and refrain from tampering or fabrication. Students bear full responsibility for all content.

III. Specific Requirements
Permitted Usage
Literature Search and Management: May assist in literature retrieval, categorization, and preliminary summarization. However, literature selection, citation, and critical analysis must be performed independently by the student.

Chart Creation Assistance: May recommend statistical chart types and assist in chart creation, but must not generate or modify original research data or key charts. Final charts must be original.

Assistance with Non-Innovative Methods: May assist in selecting or recommending non-innovative research methods, provided that students review and test such methods independently.

Reference Formatting: May assist in checking the formatting of references, but generated results must be manually verified.

Prohibited Usage
Core Content Generation: It is strictly prohibited to rely entirely on AI tools for thesis (design) writing or research. AI tools must not be used for research proposal design, innovative methodology design, algorithm framework construction, thesis structure design, data analysis, or summarizing innovations.

Data Tampering and Fabrication: It is strictly prohibited to directly use unverified AI-generated content, fabricate or falsify research data using AI tools, or tamper with experimental results, original images, etc.

Avoiding Plagiarism Detection: It is strictly prohibited to use AI tools for "paraphrasing" or "structural reorganization" of theses to evade plagiarism detection.

Handling of Confidential Content: Text or data involving privacy or confidentiality must not be uploaded to AI platforms to ensure information security.

IV. Documentation and Explanation
Students must obtain prior approval from their supervising instructor before using AI tools in their undergraduate graduation projects (theses) and adhere to relevant university and departmental regulations. The specific AI tools used and their purposes must be clearly documented in the Undergraduate Graduation Thesis (Design) AI Tool Usage Declaration Form (Attachment 1). Supervising instructors are responsible for reviewing the compliance of students’ AI tool usage to ensure it does not hinder the cultivation of innovative capabilities.

V. AIGC Detection and Violation Handling
The university provides an AIGC (Artificial Intelligence Generated Content) detection platform to assist supervising instructors in guiding graduation projects (theses). Following plagiarism detection, departments will conduct random AIGC spot checks at a rate of no less than 10%. A detected AI-generated content ratio not exceeding 40% in principle will be considered acceptable. Only after passing the AIGC detection will students be eligible for the final defense. Details regarding the AIGC detection platform will be announced separately.

For students whose detection results exceed 40%, if the department’s academic committee confirms academic misconduct, a delay of at least one month in the defense will be imposed. In severe cases, penalties will be applied in accordance with relevant regulations such as the Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics Measures for Addressing Academic Misconduct (University Research Notice [2020] No. 7) and the Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics Undergraduate Student Status Management Measures (University Academic Notice [2024] No. 30).

Attachment:
Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics Graduation Project (Thesis) AI Tool Usage Declaration Form

policy_summary:
Article 1
For the purpose of further improving degree conferral procedures, establishing scientific ethical standards and academic research norms, regulating the use of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, and avoiding risks to research integrity arising from the misuse or abuse of AI technologies, these guidelines are formulated in accordance with the principles of innovation, honesty, transparency, and responsibility. They are based on documents such as the Measures for the Prevention and Handling of Academic Misconduct in Higher Education Institutions (Ministry of Education Order No. 40) and the Guidelines for Responsible Research Conduct (2023) issued by the Ministry of Science and Technology, and are tailored to the actual conditions of the university.

Article 2
Thesis and graduation projects (designs) refer to innovative achievements completed by degree applicants, including theses completed by graduate students and graduation projects (designs) completed by undergraduate students at our university. This also encompasses reports or practical outcomes related to the degree conferral process during the writing stage, including but not limited to research proposals, pre-defense theses, and graduation projects (designs).

Article 3
Artificial intelligence technologies mentioned in these guidelines refer to tools that generate content such as text, audio, and images using AI, including but not limited to DeepSeek, ChatGPT, Kimi, Doubao, iFlytek Spark, ERNIE Bot, Tongyi Qianwen, and others.

Article 4
AI technologies may be used to track research trends, collect, and organize literature. However, the authenticity and reliability of AI-generated information must be ensured. The direct use of unverified AI-generated content such as research reports, topic suggestions, literature reviews, algorithm (model) frameworks, etc., is discouraged.

Article 5
AI technologies may be used to generate simulated data and perform statistical analysis of original data. However, the direct use of AI technologies to generate original data, experimental results, or core innovative achievements is discouraged.

Article 6
AI technologies may be used to assist in creating graphics, but the generated content must be verified and appropriately labeled. The direct use of AI to generate chart content is discouraged.

Article 7
AI technologies may be used for text proofreading and thesis format verification. However, the direct use of AI technologies to generate entire works, complete chapters, full paragraphs, or references is discouraged.

Article 8
Content completed with the assistance of AI technologies must be appropriately marked in the relevant sections of the work. The name, version, date, and usage process of the tool must be declared in sections such as notes, acknowledgments, references, or appendices.

Article 9
The use of AI technologies to process confidential content is prohibited.

Article 10
Students must submit an Artificial Intelligence Technology Use Declaration before their thesis defense. During the defense, experts should inquire about and inform students of the risks associated with AI-assisted content.

Article 11
Supervisors should strengthen guidance on the use of AI technologies and provide education on academic integrity throughout the entire training process.

Article 12
All training units should conduct educational programs and may develop internal regulations based on disciplinary characteristics and academic practices to guide students in using AI technologies honestly, transparently, and responsibly.

The undergraduate college will then uniformly upload the corresponding college-specific format template to the thesis management system.

Final Draft Review: After standardizing the thesis format, students must upload and submit the final draft of the thesis (design) to the system, awaiting review and approval by the supervising instructor.

Similarity Rate Detection: Upon approval by the supervising instructor, the thesis shall be submitted to the thesis management system for similarity rate detection.

AIGC Detection: Upon approval by the supervising instructor, the thesis shall be submitted to the thesis management system for AIGC detection.

policy_summary:
II. Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) Detection

Subjects of Detection
All 2025 graduating students will participate in AIGC detection, with each student receiving one free detection provided by their college. Detection must be completed by May 20.

Detection Content
The detection will identify whether the text partially or entirely exhibits characteristics of AI model generation, producing a detection report that includes the suspected proportion of AIGC content relative to the full text, the distribution of suspected AIGC fragments, and other relevant details.

Application of Results
The AIGC detection results will serve as a key reference for evaluating the grades of the 2025 undergraduate graduation theses (designs) and for selecting outstanding theses (designs). Students found to have violated regulations on AI tool usage will face appropriate penalties based on the severity of the offense, which may include but are not limited to disqualification from the defense, invalidation of thesis grades, and handling as cases of academic misconduct.

Other Relevant Requirements
(1) Supervising instructors must guide students in the proper use of AI tools, ensuring their usage is reasonable and compliant, and review their eligibility for defense.
(2) Reviewing instructors must evaluate the thesis (design) and provide opinions on whether the student should proceed to the defense.
(3) Defense panels must strengthen the examination and verification of core competencies and original contributions for theses (designs) suspected of improper AI tool usage.
(4) Students bear ultimate responsibility for the content generated using AI tools, ensuring it complies with academic standards and quality requirements. AI tools must not be listed as authors or collaborators.
(5) Students who violate AI tool usage regulations will face appropriate penalties based on the severity of the offense, including but not limited to disqualification from the defense, invalidation of thesis grades, and handling as cases of academic misconduct. Colleges and supervising instructors whose students are identified as having "problematic theses" due to AI tool usage in random inspections by the Ministry of Education will be dealt with according to relevant regulations.

Before the 2025 undergraduate defense, the secretary of each group must collect the AIGC detection results signed by the supervising instructor as part of the defense materials.

The results are an important reference for thesis grading and excellent thesis selection; improper AI use leads to penalties like canceled defense eligibility, and signed detection results are required as defense materials.

policy_summary:
2. Academic Integrity

All undergraduate graduation projects (theses) for the class of 2025 are required to undergo academic misconduct detection, which includes comparison against the CNKI (China National Knowledge Infrastructure) database, internal comparisons with theses from the current and previous years within the university, and AI-generated content (AIGC) detection.

In principle, the permissible similarity ratio for science and engineering disciplines must not exceed 25%, while for other disciplines it must not exceed 30%. Projects (theses) failing to meet the required similarity ratio will not be eligible to proceed to the graduation project (thesis) defense stage.

The direct use of AI-generated content for writing graduation projects (theses) is strictly prohibited. Any such practice, once identified and verified, will be classified as ghostwriting of a graduation project (thesis) and treated as academic misconduct, subject to disciplinary actions in accordance with the university's regulations. If students utilize AI writing tools for auxiliary purposes, it is recommended that they provide a clear statement detailing the usage in relevant sections of their graduation project (thesis), such as the references or acknowledgments.

Students may revise their theses based on the AI evaluation suggestions, while supervisors may use these insights to provide further guidance.

It does not influence grade assessment. The final quality of the graduation project (thesis) shall be determined and verified by the supervising instructor.

policy_summary:
(6) Plagiarism Check and AIGC Detection for Graduation Projects (Theses)

In order to further strengthen the quality supervision and management of graduation projects (theses), encourage academic innovation, uphold academic integrity, ensure the quality of undergraduate graduation projects (theses), regulate the scientific and reasonable use of AIGC technology by students, and prevent academic misconduct such as plagiarism, AI-assisted ghostwriting, falsification, and improper citation during the writing process, the university will conduct plagiarism checks and AIGC detection on the final drafts (pre-defense) and post-defense versions of the 2025 undergraduate graduation projects (theses). These measures are implemented in accordance with the Academic Degrees Law of the People’s Republic of China (Presidential Decree No. 22) and the Measures for Handling Academic Misconduct in Degree Theses (Ministry of Education Decree No. 34).

1. Subjects of Detection
All graduation projects (theses) of the 2025 undergraduate cohort.

2. Detection Standards
Colleges should further educate students on academic integrity, require them to sign the Fuzhou University Undergraduate Graduation Project (Thesis) Integrity Commitment Form, and clearly inform students of the following university-established thesis detection standards and handling measures:

(1) The comparison scope for students’ graduation projects (theses) includes the default database of the VIPS (Weipu) plagiarism detection system and all graduation projects (theses) of the current cohort.

(2) The university stipulates the following detection standards for undergraduate graduation projects (theses):

The similarity ratio after removing the student’s own previously published work should be ≤20%.

The AIGC full-text detection value should be ≤20%.

Colleges may establish stricter detection standards based on disciplinary characteristics and graduation project (thesis) requirements. These standards must be communicated to students before the start of the graduation project (thesis) work and submitted to the Academic Affairs Office for record-keeping. Students’ graduation projects (theses) must meet the college’s detection standards to be eligible for defense.

(3) The plagiarism check files (pre-defense and post-defense versions) for undergraduate graduation projects (theses) must be uploaded in PDF format.

(4) For university-level outstanding graduation projects (theses), the detection results should meet the following criteria:

The similarity ratio after removing the student’s own previously published work should be ≤15%.

The AIGC full-text detection value should be ≤15%.

If either the similarity ratio after removal or the AIGC full-text detection value exceeds 15%, the qualification for university-level outstanding evaluation will be revoked.
Rationale:
Check framework is coded as 1: The university explicitly requires AIGC detection for graduation theses. The university will conduct AIGC detection on both the final draft before defense and the final draft after defense of all 2025 undergraduate graduation theses, with clear detection objects and implementation procedures, forming a formal mandatory AIGC detection requirement.
Tool specified is coded as 0: The university does not specifies a specific AIGC detection tool. 


Linked to pass is coded as 1: AIGC detection results directly affect thesis approval, defense eligibility or degree conferral. The university sets an AIGC full-text detection threshold of ≤20%, and students must meet the college's detection standards (which may be stricter) to participate in the defense; for university-level excellent theses, the AIGC threshold is ≤15%, and those exceeding it will be disqualified from the selection.

policy_summary:
IV. Assessment and Handling of Detection Results

Procedures for Handling Detection Results:
(1) If the detection result R ≤ 20%, it is deemed to have passed the detection, and the student is eligible for the defense.
(2) If the final detection result R > 20%, it is deemed to have failed the detection, and the student is not eligible for the defense.
(Note: R refers to the percentage of text similarity, calculated as the proportion of overlapping text between the submitted graduation thesis/design and non-original academic works relative to the full length of the thesis.)

For graduation theses/designs with a final detection result R ≤ 20% that require a second defense, a re-detection must be conducted before the second defense. The thesis/design may proceed to the second defense only after passing the re-detection.

The detection result for university-level outstanding graduation theses/designs must not exceed 10%.

This round of detection includes an AIGC (Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content) detection component. If the AIGC detection result exceeds 40%, it is recommended that the thesis/design be revised.

All undergraduate graduation theses/designs must undergo detection. Students unable to submit detection materials on time must submit a written explanation (see Attachment 2; please email the electronic version to songjia@surrey.ac.uk) at least five working days before the detection deadline. The explanation must be approved by the supervising instructor and the college, then submitted to the Academic Affairs Office for review. Students who fail to submit detection materials without valid reasons or do not participate in the detection on time will be disqualified from the graduation thesis defense for the current year.

policy_summary:
(II) AIGC (Artificial Intelligence Generated Content) Detection

AIGC detection refers to the identification and verification of content generated by artificial intelligence.

Number of Detections: The system provides each student with 2 detection opportunities.

Detection Standards: The proportion of AI-generated content indicated by the AIGC detection results should, in principle, not exceed 40% (inclusive).

Detection Process: After logging into the graduation project management system, click "Process Management" → "Submit AIGC Detection" → upload the thesis (detailed instructions are provided in Appendix 2). Both the college and the supervising instructor can view the detection results in the system.

Note: If a student's AIGC detection results exceed the specified threshold during the defense, the college will submit the case to the defense panel for review. If academic misconduct is confirmed, penalties may include delayed defense, redoing the graduation project (thesis), or other measures as appropriate. In severe cases, the student's grade will be recorded as failing.
Rationale: Check framework is coded as 1: The university explicitly requires AIGC detection for undergraduate graduation theses, as it specifies 2 free detection opportunities per student, a clear passing threshold (AI-generated content ratio ≤ 40%), a standardized operation process via the graduation project management system, and penalty measures for non-compliance.Tool specified is coded as 1: The university specifies a specific AIGC detection tool, explicitly stating that the detection relies on the Weipu AIGC detection system.Linked to pass is coded as 1: AIGC detection results directly affect undergraduate thesis defense eligibility, academic misconduct identification and score evaluation, as students exceeding the 40% threshold will be reviewed by the defense panel; those deemed to have committed academic misconduct may face delayed defense, thesis revision, or a failing grade.

policy_summary:
III. Detection Process
(1) AIGC Detection and Format Checking

AIGC detection is used to identify whether the text partially or entirely exhibits characteristics of being generated by artificial intelligence models. It produces a detection report containing metrics such as the suspected proportion of AIGC content relative to the full text and the distribution of suspected AIGC segments. Format checking is conducted to verify whether the thesis (design) complies with the Nanjing Forestry University Undergraduate Thesis (Design) Writing Standards and Formatting Guidelines (Attachment 1).

Both AIGC detection and format checking are performed within the graduation thesis (design) system (specific operational procedures are detailed in Attachment 2). Under the guidance of their advisors, students are required to revise and improve their theses (designs) based on the results of the AIGC detection and format checks. The AIGC detection results will serve as an important reference for evaluating the grades of the 2025 undergraduate theses and for selecting outstanding theses.

policy_summary:
II. AIGC Detection Requirements

In principle, all graduation projects (theses) must undergo AIGC detection. The main body, abstract, and acknowledgments are mandatory components for detection. Supervising instructors may require the inclusion of process materials such as research proposals and foreign language translations within the detection scope.

Each college may specify detailed AIGC detection criteria based on the characteristics of their respective disciplines and majors. These criteria shall be submitted to the Academic Affairs Office for filing before implementation. The AIGC detection results will serve as a reference for evaluating graduation project (thesis) grades and selecting outstanding undergraduate graduation projects (theses).

For graduation projects (theses) with a significant proportion of suspected AI-generated content in the detection results, supervising instructors shall review and verify the findings, guiding and urging students to make revisions and undergo re-detection. Cases confirmed as academic misconduct will be subject to appropriate penalties, such as postponement of the defense or redoing the graduation project (thesis). In severe cases, graduation eligibility may be revoked, and the grade for the graduation project (thesis) will be recorded as failing.
Rationale: Check framework is coded as 1: The university explicitly requires AIGC detection for graduation theses, as it mandates AIGC detection for all graduation theses in principle, specifies mandatory detection contents (main text, abstract, acknowledgment) and optional process materials, and allows colleges to formulate specific standards based on discipline characteristics; Tool specified is coded as 0: The university does not specify any specific AIGC detection tool, only stating detection requirements and follow-up measures without naming or indicating a particular tool (e.g., CNKI AI Check, Weipu AIGC detection); Linked to pass is coded as 1: AIGC detection results directly affect thesis approval, defense eligibility, graduation qualification and score evaluation, as a large proportion of suspected AI-generated content requires revision and re-inspection, and those constituting academic misconduct may face penalties such as delayed defense, redoing the thesis, canceled graduation qualification, or a failing score, with the results also serving as a reference for score evaluation and excellent thesis selection.

policy_summary:
2. AIGC Standards

The proportion of AI-generated content indicated in the AIGC detection results shall be ≤40%. If the AIGC detection result of a thesis exceeds 40%, the following procedures shall be implemented:

(1) Warning and Reminder:
The secondary college shall issue a warning to students whose AIGC detection results exceed the standard, alerting them to the high proportion of AI-generated content in their graduation thesis (design). The students shall be required to conduct self-inspection and correction under the guidance of their instructors and make necessary revisions to their graduation thesis (design).

(2) Revision and Re-examination:
After receiving the warning, students must carefully revise their graduation thesis (design) to reduce the proportion of AI-generated content. Upon completion of the revision, the updated graduation thesis (design) shall be resubmitted to the secondary college, which will organize a re-examination.

(3) Optimization of Detection Requirements:
Based on the reference standards for AIGC detection results, secondary colleges may establish discipline-specific standards that align with the characteristics of their respective majors and the actual conditions of the college. These standards shall be submitted to the Academic Affairs Office for review and approval before implementation.

(4) Strengthening Academic Integrity Education:
In cases where AIGC limits are exceeded, secondary colleges shall enhance academic integrity education, raise students’ awareness of academic norms and ethics, guide students to establish correct academic values, and prevent the occurrence of academic misconduct.

If students or instructors raise objections to the detection results, the graduation thesis (design) leadership group of the secondary college shall organize experts for evaluation and issue a conclusion.
Rationale: Check framework is coded as 1: The university explicitly requires AIGC detection for graduation theses, as it sets a clear AIGC detection standard (full-text AI-generated content ratio ≤ 40%), formulates a complete process for exceeding the standard (warning, revision, re-inspection), and clarifies the authority for formulating professional standards and handling objections; Tool specified is coded as 0: The university does not specify any specific AIGC detection tool, only stipulating the detection standard and follow-up handling measures without naming or indicating a particular tool (e.g., CNKI AI Check, Weipu AIGC detection); Linked to pass is coded as 1: AIGC detection results directly affect thesis approval and defense eligibility, as exceeding the 40% threshold requires mandatory revision and re-inspection, and failure to meet the standard after handling will affect the progress of the thesis (design) and subsequent defense procedures.

policy_summary:
V. Similarity Detection and AIGC Detection

The university will conduct similarity detection and AIGC detection for all undergraduate graduation projects (theses) and provide a unified detection platform. Each college is responsible for organizing and implementing the similarity detection and AIGC detection for graduation projects (theses).

Each college must organize faculty and students involved in graduation projects (theses) to thoroughly study the Measures for Handling Academic Misconduct in Degree Theses (Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China Order No. 34) (Attachment 5) and the Measures for the Prevention and Handling of Academic Misconduct in Higher Education Institutions (Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China Order No. 40) (Attachment 6).

Each college must organize students participating in the 2025 graduation projects (theses) to sign the Academic Integrity Commitment Form (Attachment 7). The form must be signed by the student personally in duplicate: one copy will be retained by the college, and the other must be bound by the student in their graduation project (thesis) (placed before the task description). The signed Academic Integrity Commitment Form must be submitted to the respective college by November 29, 2024.

Once similarity detection and AIGC detection are submitted, the thesis title cannot be changed after review.

Similarity detection and AIGC detection are scheduled to take place from April 22 to May 5, 2025.

If the similarity detection shows a duplication rate higher than 20% or the AIGC detection rate exceeds 30%, the student will not be eligible to apply for the defense. The university provides two detection opportunities for the defense draft. If the second detection still fails, the overall grade for the graduation project (thesis) will be recorded as failing, and the student must retake the course. For university-level outstanding graduation projects (theses), the duplication rate should in principle be less than 10%. Each college may establish stricter detection standards based on their specific circumstances.
